name: Banba
description: Banba is one of the three eponymous goddess figures of Ireland, along with Fódla and Ériu. These goddesses represent the land of Ireland and are often associated with sovereignty and fertility. Banba is considered a powerful figure representing the ancient connection between the Irish people and their land. She is said to be one of the daughters of Ernmas of the Tuatha Dé Danann. When the Milesians arrived in Ireland, Banba, along with her sisters, requested that the island be named after her. While Ériu ultimately lent her name to the island (Éire being derived from her name), Banba and Fódla are still used poetically as names for Ireland. Banba's role is strongly connected to the land and the power of the land to sustain its people. She represents the ancient pre-Christian beliefs and the enduring spirit of Ireland. She is a symbol of national identity and the deep-rooted connection to the ancestral homeland. Her power is tied to the fertility of the land and the protection of its people. She is invoked in poetry and literature as a personification of Ireland itself, embodying its beauty, strength, and resilience. She is often depicted as a beautiful and regal woman, radiating the power of the land.
subtitle: Eponymous Goddess of Ireland

keyMyths.title: Arrival of the Milesians
keyMyths.description: When the Milesians arrived, Banba, along with her sisters, met them and requested that the island be named after her. Although Ériu's name was ultimately chosen, Banba's name remains a poetic synonym for Ireland.

keyMyths.title: Daughter of Ernmas
keyMyths.description: Banba is said to be one of the daughters of Ernmas, a powerful figure within the Tuatha Dé Danann. This lineage connects her to the divine and magical origins of the Irish people.

keyMyths.title: Goddess of Sovereignty
keyMyths.description: Banba's role as an eponymous goddess links her directly to the sovereignty of Ireland. Her blessing was sought to ensure the rightful rule of the land.

sources.author: Geoffrey Keating
